papers.title: Aligning Attention with Human Rationales for Self-Explaining Hate Speech Detection
claims_supported_text: Primary competitor: SRA uses softmax + KL alignment loss on HateXplain; claims 2.4x better explainability vs baselines
papers.notes: CRITICAL — must differentiate from SRA on structural sparsity, faithfulness claim strength, and sufficiency

papers.title: HateXplain: A Benchmark Dataset for Explainable Hate Speech Detection
claims_supported_text: 20K posts, 3-class labels, rationale span annotations, avg 5.47 tokens highlighted per post (~25% of avg 23.42 tokens)
papers.notes: Primary dataset; rationale annotations are direct supervision signal for sparsemax

papers.title: From Softmax to Sparsemax: A Sparse Model of Attention and Multi-Label Classification
claims_supported_text: Sparsemax = Euclidean projection onto probability simplex; exactly-zero probabilities; differentiable; efficient Jacobian
papers.notes: Core technical component; must cite as foundation for sparsemax mechanism

papers.title: Adaptively Sparse Transformers
claims_supported_text: α-entmax: learnable sparsity parameter per head; no accuracy cost on MT; improved interpretability and head specialization
papers.notes: Extension to consider: learn α per head with rationale supervision as signal

papers.title: Attention is not Explanation
claims_supported_text: Attention weights frequently disagree with gradient importance; adversarial permutations produce different attention with identical predictions
papers.notes: Motivates supervised attention; our approach goes beyond post-hoc explanation

papers.title: Attention is not not Explanation
claims_supported_text: Attention CAN serve as explanation under appropriate conditions; proposes 4 tests including end-to-end adversarial training
papers.notes: Counterpoint to Jain & Wallace; our work sidesteps debate by making faithfulness structural

papers.title: ERASER: A Benchmark to Evaluate Rationalized NLP Models
claims_supported_text: Standardized comprehensiveness (AOPC), sufficiency (AOPC), plausibility (F1 vs human rationales) metrics across 8 NLP datasets
papers.notes: Primary evaluation framework for faithfulness metrics; mini-project found comprehensiveness gains

papers.title: A Rationale-Guided Multi-Task Learning Framework for Hate Speech Detection (RISE)
claims_supported_text: Multi-task: HSD main + rationale tagging (BIO) auxiliary; emoji semantics module; outperforms SOTA
papers.notes: Adjacent competitor: MTL approach vs. attention supervision approach; does not use sparsemax

papers.title: IvRA: Interpretability-Driven Training for Attention Explanations
claims_supported_text: Attention regularization for simulatability, faithfulness, consistency — most similar to our loss design

papers.title: HateCOT: Explanation-Enhanced Dataset for Generalizable Offensive Speech
claims_supported_text: Chain-of-thought rationales via LLM for generalization; different approach to explanation from attention supervision
papers.notes: Generalization baseline; different mechanism (LLM COT vs. attention supervision)

papers.title: Hate Speech Detection with Generalizable Target-aware Fairness
claims_supported_text: Fairness-aware objective on HateXplain; generalizable target-aware fairness is open problem
papers.notes: Relevant to fairness evaluation; SRA claims fairness improvements

papers.title: Sparse Sequence-to-Sequence Models
claims_supported_text: α-entmax family for sparse MT alignments
papers.notes: Part of DeepSPIN sparse attention family

papers.title: Learning from Sufficient Rationales
claims_supported_text: Sufficiency ≠ token classification; highly informative rationales not always sufficient — complicates evaluation story
papers.notes: Important for framing sufficiency results; may explain inconsistent mini-project sufficiency

papers.title: Regularization, Semi-supervision, Supervision for Plausible Attention
claims_supported_text: Three strategies for attention plausibility in classification tasks
papers.notes: Direct prior work on attention supervision strategies

papers.title: HuMAL: Aligning Human and Machine Attention
claims_supported_text: Cosine similarity alignment loss between human and machine attention
papers.notes: Alternative alignment loss formulation to compare against

papers.title: Towards Faithfully Interpretable NLP Systems
claims_supported_text: Defines faithfulness; distinguishes faithfulness from plausibility
papers.notes: Definitional paper for faithfulness vs plausibility distinction

papers.title: Automated Hate Speech Detection and the Problem of Offensive Language
claims_supported_text: 24K tweets; hate/offensive/normal classification; widely used baseline dataset
papers.notes: Standard baseline dataset for cross-dataset generalization eval

papers.title: Sparse and Constrained Attention for Neural Machine Translation
claims_supported_text: Constrained sparsemax with upper-bound fertility constraints for NMT coverage; differentiable and sparse
papers.cross_field_note: Uses constrained sparsemax for NMT coverage problem. Same base mechanism (sparsemax) but different constraint (fertility budget vs human rationale mask), different task (generation vs classification), different evaluation. Must be cited and differentiated in related work.

papers.title: Guiding Visual Question Answering with Attention Priors
claims_supported_text: Uses grounding annotation priors to refine VQA model attention; improves interpretability and sample efficiency
papers.cross_field_note: Annotation-supervised soft attention for VQA; same high-level idea (annotation guides attention) but uses soft priors, not sparsemax; vision domain; ERASER metrics not applicable.

papers.title: Self-Explaining Hate Speech Detection with Moral Rationales
claims_supported_text: SMRA: supervised attention alignment using Moral Foundations Theory moral rationale spans; softmax; Portuguese HateBRMoralXplain; IoU F1 +7.4pp, Token F1 +5.0pp, sufficiency +2.3pp
papers.notes: Second concurrent work; same family as SRA; uses moral MFT spans not crowd-annotated HateXplain rationales; Portuguese not English; softmax not sparsemax. Strengthens cite-and-differentiate posture but not kill signal.
